headline: Subsidy Bill Reaches RM82B, One-Third of Federal Revenue
context: The Finance Ministry disclosed that total subsidies, incentives, and social assistance reached RM82 billion in 2025, consuming 33% of federal revenue and crowding out development spending on infrastructure, education, and healthcare.

cards.text: RM82 billion in subsidies. Malaysia spends more propping up prices than building roads, schools, and hospitals combined.
cards.sub: Development expenditure at RM72 billion is RM10 billion less than the subsidy bill for the first time

cards.h: Fuel subsidies at RM38 billion account for 46% of total subsidies. T20 households capture 42% of fuel subsidy benefits.
cards.s: Malaysia's subsidy-to-GDP ratio at 5.1% exceeds Indonesia's 1.8% and Thailand's 1.2% after their reforms
cards.d: Fuel subsidies at RM38 billion account for 46% of total subsidies. T20 households capture 42% of fuel subsidy benefits. Malaysia's subsidy-to-GDP ratio at 5.1% exceeds Indonesia's 1.8% and Thailand's 1.2% after their reforms

cards.h: RM82 billion could fund 328,000 affordable homes or 14,000 rural clinics if redirected from blanket to targeted subsidies
cards.s: Opportunity cost is invisible. Malaysians don't see the hospitals that were never built because subsidies consumed the budget.
cards.d: RM82 billion could fund 328,000 affordable homes or 14,000 rural clinics if redirected from blanket to targeted subsidies Opportunity cost is invisible. Malaysians don't see the hospitals that were never built because subsidies consumed the budget.

cards.h: No single ministry tracks total subsidy expenditure; figure compiled from 8 separate budget line items across 6 ministries
cards.s: Subsidy Rationalisation Unit promised in 2023 still not operational. Reform constantly deferred before elections.
cards.d: No single ministry tracks total subsidy expenditure; figure compiled from 8 separate budget line items across 6 ministries Subsidy Rationalisation Unit promised in 2023 still not operational. Reform constantly deferred before elections.

cards.text: Spending RM82 billion to keep prices low while infrastructure crumbles is borrowing from the future to subsidise the present
Like a household that spends a third of its income keeping grocery prices low while the roof leaks. Eventually the roof collapses.
cards.sub: Subsidising consumption at the expense of infrastructure investment creates compounding long-term costs

cards.text: Should Malaysia legislate a subsidy cap at 20% of revenue with mandatory phase-down schedules for each subsidy category?

confidence: Low — derived from published cards, original brief not on disk; submit for retroactive Stage 2/3 verification
